questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, rather than acting on Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions. The safer answer is Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95% because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer the focus, without accounting for Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.
distractorRationales.d: The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min is not the best answer, even though the higher-risk finding is work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95% targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
visualRationale.conclusion: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing vital signs in the pediatric unit. Which set of vital signs is within normal limits for a healthy 3-year-old toddler?
questions.rationale: Normal vital signs for a 3-year-old toddler: HR 80–130 bpm, RR 22–37 breaths/min, BP approximately 90–100/55–65 mmHg. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 are all within expected ranges. Choice A has a bradycardic HR and adult-level BP. Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress, and RR 40 is elevated. Choice D has significant bradycardia. Key developmental milestone: vital signs normalize toward adult ranges with age — neonates have the highest HR and RR, lowest BP.

distractorRationales.a: The problem with HR 68, RR 14, BP 120/80 is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, while the decisive cue is toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37. That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: HR 145, RR 40, BP 80/48 fits a lower-risk interpretation, instead of responding to toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward HR 55, RR 18, BP 110/72, and misses that Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.
visualRationale.conclusion: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy at 8:00 AM. Per current ASA NPO guidelines, what are the correct restrictions for solid food and clear liquids?

options.text: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid
Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery
Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, clear juice, black coffee) allowed until 2 hours before
No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk

questions.rationale: Current ASA NPO guidelines (2017, updated 2023): Solid foods, fried/fatty meals: NPO ≥8 hours. Light meals (toast, clear liquids): NPO ≥6 hours. Non-clear liquids including milk and formula: NPO ≥6 hours. Clear liquids (water, apple juice without pulp, clear carbonated beverages, black coffee or tea without cream, sports drinks): may be consumed up to 2 hours before elective procedures. Chewing gum and hard candy: treat as clear liquid — stop 2 hours before. These evidence-based guidelines replaced the outdated 'NPO after midnight' rule. The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance, and perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction.

distractorRationales.a: This choice focuses on Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid, despite the clinical warning in The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water,, not a step that delays stabilization of unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.b: The problem with Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, instead of responding to ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before. That mismatch risks slower recognition of unstable glucose physiology and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk as the key issue, although the nurse should be guided by The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization and leave unstable glucose physiology unresolved.

questions.takeaway: ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before; clear liquids until 2 hrs before. 'NPO after midnight' is outdated and harmful. Clear liquids: water, clear juice (no pulp), black coffee/tea.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type 1 diabetes before breakfast. Which action by the nurse requires IMMEDIATE correction?

options.text: Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe
Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration
Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal
Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. It should NEVER be mixed with any other insulin, including rapid-acting insulins. Mixing glargine with other insulins alters its pH, causing it to crystallize and unpredictably changes both insulins' absorption profiles. The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Each insulin must be drawn in a separate syringe and administered as separate injections. Drawing up separately is correct. Injecting aspart before meals (pre-prandial) is appropriate. Site rotation is correct practice.

distractorRationales.a: The clinical error in Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type. The correct path is Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.c: Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on It has its own pH and formulation. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization and leave unstable glucose physiology unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh is not the best answer, even though the higher-risk finding is A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization while unstable glucose physiology progresses.

questions.takeaway: Glargine (Lantus) = NEVER MIX with other insulins. It has its own pH and formulation. Mix-up can cause crystal formation and unpredictable glycemic effect. Use separate syringes, separate injections. Applies also to detemir (Levemir).

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for four patients on a medical-surgical unit. Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL
A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10
A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights
A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due

questions.rationale: The patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness, and brain injury. The other patients represent important but lower-acuity concerns: the post-op patient has manageable pain, the heart failure patient's weight gain needs assessment but is not immediately life-threatening, and the UTI patient's antibiotic timing is a scheduled task.

distractorRationales.b: This choice focuses on A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10, instead of responding to Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of unstable glucose physiology and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights, although the nurse should be guided by Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The priority is A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of, not a step that delays stabilization of unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.d: The problem with A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, while the decisive cue is The patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening. The priority is A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of, not a step that delays stabilization of unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine monitoring, and informational questions.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a ventilated patient wants to implement the VAP prevention bundle. Which combination of interventions is CORRECT according to current evidence?

options.text: HOB flat, scheduled oral care q8h with water, continuous sedation infusion
HOB 30–45°, oral care with 0.12% chlorhexidine q6h, daily sedation vacation with SBT
HOB 30–45°, oral care q12h with water, sedation vacation only when FiO2 <0.40
HOB 15°, chlorhexidine oral care q8h, continuous sedation to prevent self-extubation

questions.rationale: The VAP prevention bundle includes: (1) HOB elevation 30–45° to reduce aspiration of gastric contents; (2) oral decontamination with 0.12% chlorhexidine gluconate every 6 hours to reduce oropharyngeal bacterial burden; (3) daily sedation interruption (sedation vacation) — allows neurological assessment and reduces days on ventilator; (4) daily spontaneous breathing trials (SBT) to assess readiness for extubation — the most effective strategy for reducing VAP is minimizing ventilator days.

distractorRationales.a: The problem with HOB flat, scheduled oral care q8h with water, continuous sedation infusion is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, while the decisive cue is (4) daily spontaneous breathing trials (SBT) to assess readiness for extubation — the most effective strategy for reducing. The nurse should prioritize HOB 30–45°, oral care with 0.12% chlorhexidine q6h, daily sedation vacation with SBT; otherwise oxygenation or ventilation remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes HOB 30–45°, oral care q12h with water, sedation vacation only when FiO2 <0.40 the focus, but the stem cue is (3) daily sedation interruption (sedation vacation) — allows neurological assessment and reduces days on ventilator. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so HOB 30–45°, oral care with 0.12% chlorhexidine q6h, daily sedation vacation with SBT is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: Selecting HOB 15°, chlorhexidine oral care q8h, continuous sedation to prevent self-extubation would be unsafe, rather than acting on VAP bundle: HOB 30–45°, chlorhexidine oral care, daily sedation vacation + SBT. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The priority is HOB 30–45°, oral care with 0.12% chlorhexidine q6h, daily sedation vacation with SBT, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: VAP bundle: HOB 30–45°, chlorhexidine oral care, daily sedation vacation + SBT. Each element is independently evidence-based.

questions.stem: A grieving client says: 'I feel so alone since my husband died. No one understands what I'm going through.' The nurse responds: 'Your family loves you and I'm sure they understand.' This response is an example of which non-therapeutic technique?

options.text: Giving advice
False reassurance
Changing the subject
Requesting an explanation

questions.rationale: False reassurance involves making statements that minimize the client's experience with unfounded comfort ('I'm sure they understand'). It closes the therapeutic dialogue and invalidates the client's real emotional experience. Giving advice (A) involves telling the client what to do. Changing the subject (C) would involve redirecting to a different topic. Requesting an explanation (D) would be asking 'why,' which can feel accusatory.

distractorRationales.a: The clinical error in Giving advice is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for therapeutic communication safety. The correct path is False reassurance, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Changing the subject the focus, but the stem cue is therapeutic communication safety. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is False reassurance, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Requesting an explanation is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that therapeutic communication safety. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for bedside safety action while clinical safety risk progresses.

questions.takeaway: False reassurance minimizes the client's feelings with unfounded optimism — always acknowledge feelings before offering any reframe.

questions.stem: A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Which example correctly illustrates primary prevention?

options.text: Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40
Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season
Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture
Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps

questions.rationale: Primary prevention: actions taken before disease occurs to prevent onset. Examples: immunization, health education, smoking cessation counseling, fluoridated water, condom use. Secondary prevention: early detection of existing disease (screening) — mammography, colonoscopy, blood pressure screening, Pap smear. Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease management. Mammography and colonoscopy are secondary prevention (detecting disease before symptoms). Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention.

distractorRationales.a: Selecting Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40 would be unsafe, instead of responding to A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of circulation or perfusion and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture as the key issue, although the nurse should be guided by A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The safer answer is Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season because delaying circulation and perfusion support can worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps would not address the priority, and misses that Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Primary prevention = before disease (vaccines, education); Secondary = early detection (screening); Tertiary = limit disability/restore function (rehab). Not to be confused with clinical levels of care.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: Age >65 and female gender is not the best answer, and misses that The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.c: Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29 is not the best answer, and misses that The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion is that it normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, when the bedside priority is driven by The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls. It is less defensible because History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: This option overweights Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea, while the decisive cue is Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The priority is Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, and misses that UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching. The correct path is Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: Selecting Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care would be unsafe, despite the clinical warning in Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so The priority is Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.
